title: Mental cruelty as a divorce ground - recent trends in judgments
p1: Many people believe divorce for mental cruelty requires dramatic incidents or extreme behaviour. But recent judgments show that the law is looking beyond visible harm and focusing on the emotional reality of a marriage. The question is no longer only, “What happened?” but also, “Can this relationship reasonably continue?”
p2: A surprising trend is that courts have recognised that repeated humiliation, constant emotional neglect, false allegations, or creating an unbearable environment may amount to mental cruelty in appropriate cases. At the same time, ordinary disagreements and differences between spouses are not automatically treated as cruelty. The focus remains on the impact of conduct and the overall circumstances.
p3: The key takeaway is that mental cruelty is not a fixed checklist. Courts assess patterns, behaviour, intent, and the effect on the spouse’s well-being. Recent decisions reflect a more practical approach, balancing individual dignity with the reality of married life. A single argument may not matter, but a repeated pattern can change the legal outcome.
conclusion: Do you think courts should expand the understanding of mental cruelty as society’s expectations from relationships evolve?
